identity.name: Silas Crowder
identity.bio:
Silas Crowder didn't pick up a guitar until he was nineteen, and by then his hands were already ruined the way only rope and cold water can ruin a man. Born in a shingled cottage on the Maine coast, he shipped out on herring boats at fifteen and spent the next twenty years hauling nets through the North Atlantic dark. He learned to sing to keep the watch awake, trading old dockside shanties with men twice his age, and somewhere in those long wet nights the songs turned into something of his own—stories about the widows waiting on the shore and the salt that never washes out from under your nails.

When his knees gave out and a winter storm took two men he'd fished beside for a decade, Silas came ashore for good in 1971. He carried a battered six-string into the smoky back rooms of harbor-town taverns and started playing for the price of a whiskey. His voice—gravel dragged over broken glass, damaged by decades of shouting into the wind—stopped conversations cold. A traveling producer heard him one foggy November night and put him on tape the following spring, capturing that room-warm, all-analog sound with barely more than a guitar, an upright bass, and Silas telling the truth.

He never chased fame, never smoothed the edges off a single word. His records sit somewhere between outlaw country's mean honesty and the mournful folk of the men he buried at sea. Silas Crowder sings like a man who knows exactly what the ocean takes and never gives back—and he means every word.
